1: wayhî ʾaḥar haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh ḥāṭĕʾû mašqēh melek-miṣrayim wĕhāʾōpeh laʾăḏōnêhem lĕmelek miṣrāyim — "Some time later, the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt offended their master." Mašqeh (H4945, cupbearer/butler — one who causes to drink, from šāqāh, H8248, to give drink) — the royal cupbearer was a high-ranking official in the ancient Near East, responsible for tasting and serving the king's wine and often serving as personal confidant (Nehemiah was a cupbearer to the Persian king, Neh 1:11). ʾōpeh (H644, baker, Qal participle of ʾāpāh, to bake) — another high-ranking court official. Both men's offense (ḥāṭĕʾû, from ḥāṭāʾ, to sin/offend) is unspecified — the narrative interest is in their dreams, not their crimes.
2: wayyiqṣōp parʿōh ʿal šĕnê sārîsāyw ʿal śar hammašqîm wĕʿal śar hāʾōpîm — "Pharaoh was angry (wayyiqṣōp) with his two officials, the chief cupbearer and the chief baker." Qāṣap (H7107, to be furious, to be angry) — a strong anger-verb; the same word used for Moses's anger at the Israelites returning from battle (Num 31:14) and for God's anger in prophetic contexts (Isa 8:21; 47:6). The elevating of "chief" (śar) for both officials distinguishes them from lesser staff — these are senior court officers, which explains both their access to the royal prison facility and their dream's connection to Pharaoh's fate.
3: wayyittēn ʾōtām bĕmiš̀mar bêt śar haṭṭabbāḥîm ʾel-bêt hassohar mĕqôm ʾăšer yôsēp āsûr šām — "He put them in custody in the house of the captain of the guard, in the same prison where Joseph was confined." The geographical identification — the same prison where Joseph is (mĕqôm ʾăšer yôsēp āsûr šām) — is the providential coincidence of the narrative. The captain of the guard's facility is Potiphar's household facility: this is the same man who imprisoned Joseph (39:1). The narrative loop closes: the man who imprisoned Joseph now houses the two officials who will connect Joseph to Pharaoh.
4: wayyipqōḏ śar haṭṭabbāḥîm ʾet-yôsēp ʾittām wayyĕšāret ʾōtām wayyihyû yāmîm bĕmišmar — "The captain of the guard assigned them to Joseph, and he attended them (wayyĕšāret). They remained in custody for some time." Wayyĕšāret (Piel of šārat, H8334, to serve/minister) — the same term used for Joseph's service to Potiphar (39:4). The assignment is casual from the captain's perspective; from the narrative's perspective it is the mechanism by which Joseph encounters the officials. Yāmîm (days/a period of time) — the indefinite duration in custody sets up the night of the dreams.
5: wayyaḥălĕmû ḥălôm šĕnêhem ʾîš ḥălōmô bĕlaylāh ʾeḥāḏ ʾîš kĕpitrôn ḥălōmô hammašqeh wĕhāʾōpeh ʾăšer lĕmelek miṣrāyim ʾăšer ʾăsûrîm bĕḇêt hassohar — "Each of the two men — the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt — had a dream the same night, each with its own interpretation (pitrôn)." Pitrôn (H6623, interpretation/explanation, from pātar, H6622, to interpret/solve) — the interpretation is described as inherent to the dream: each dream has its own pitrôn. The word pātar appears only in the Joseph narrative in the OT; it is possibly an Egyptian loanword for the professional dream-interpretation (ptr in Egyptian). Joseph will claim in v8 that pitrōnîm belong to God — using the very professional vocabulary to deny the professional claim.
6: wayyāḇōʾ ʾălêhem yôsēp babbōqer wayyarʾ ʾōtām wĕhinnām zōʿăpîm — "When Joseph came to them in the morning, he saw they were dejected (zōʿăpîm)." Zōʿăpîm (H2196, Qal participle plural of zāʿap, to be troubled/gloomy/sad) — this word for downcast appearance occurs only here in the Pentateuch; it describes a visible emotional state that Joseph reads on their faces. Joseph's attentiveness to the emotional state of those in his care — asking "why do you look so sad?" (v7) rather than proceeding to his duties — is a mark of the pastoral-administrator figure he has become.
7: wayyišʾal ʾet-sārîsê parʿōh ʾăšer ʾittô bĕmiš̀mar bêt ʾăḏōnāyw lēʾmōr maddûaʿ pĕnêḵem rāʿîm hayyôm — "He asked Pharaoh's officials who were in custody with him, 'Why are your faces downcast (rāʿîm) today?'" Pĕnêḵem rāʿîm (your faces are evil/downcast) — raʿ (H7451, bad/evil/sad) applied to facial appearance; the same word used for Er's wickedness (38:7) and later for the seven gaunt cows of Pharaoh's dream (41:3). Joseph's direct question rather than passive observation is a narrative signal: he is actively engaging with those in his care. This pastoral attentiveness is the condition for the dream-disclosure that will follow.
8: wayyōʾmĕrû ʾēlāyw ḥălôm ḥālamnu ûpōtēr ʾên ʾōtô wayyōʾmer ʾălêhem yôsēp hălôʾ lēʾlōhîm pitrōnîm sappĕrû-nāʾ lî — "They answered, 'We have had dreams and there is no one to interpret them.' Joseph said, 'Do not interpretations belong to God (hălôʾ lēʾlōhîm pitrōnîm)? Tell me your dreams.'" The theological claim is the interpretive key to the entire dream-sequence. Hălôʾ lēʾlōhîm pitrōnîm — the rhetorical question expects "yes" — interpretations belong to God, not to professional dream-interpreters. Joseph refuses the position of wise-man-for-hire (which Egyptian dream-interpretation was) and instead attributes interpretation to divine disclosure. This places him in the prophetic tradition rather than the mantic tradition: he does not decode symbols by professional expertise but receives revelation. The claim is borne out when God gives the interpretation (vv12-19 show Joseph receiving, not calculating, the meaning).
9: wayyĕsappēr śar-hammašqîm ʾet-ḥălōmô lĕyôsēp wayyōʾmer lô baḥălōmî wĕhinnēh-gefen lĕpānāy — "So the chief cupbearer told Joseph his dream: 'In my dream I saw a vine in front of me.'" The vine (gefen, H1612) — a central symbol of covenant blessing in the OT (Ps 128:3: "your wife like a fruitful vine"; Isa 5:1-7: YHWH's vineyard as Israel; Mic 4:4: "each man under his vine and fig tree"). In the cupbearer's dream, the vine is the source of Pharaoh's cup — the productive symbol that defines the cupbearer's entire vocational existence. The dream's simplicity — vine, branches, grapes, cup, Pharaoh's hand — maps directly onto the cupbearer's daily professional actions.
10: ûḇaggepen šĕlōšāh śārîgîm wĕhîʾ kĕporāḥat ʿālĕtāh niṣṭāh hēḇîʾû ʾašĕkĕlōtêyhā ʿănāḇîm — "On the vine were three branches. As it budded, it blossomed, and its clusters ripened into grapes." The three branches (šĕlōšāh śārîgîm) develop in accelerated sequence — bud, blossom, ripe grapes — suggesting a compressed time-vision rather than ordinary agricultural time. The acceleration from bud to harvest in the dream-vision is the narrative signal that an interpretation is required: ordinary vines do not compress agricultural seasons into a single moment. ʿĂnāḇîm (grapes, H6025) — the specific fruit that will be expressed into Pharaoh's cup.
11: wĕkôs parʿōh bĕyāḏî wāʾeqqaḥ ʾet-hāʿănāḇîm wāʾeqōṣ ʾōtām ʾel-kôs parʿōh wāʾettēn ʾet-hakôs ʿal-kap parʿōh — "Pharaoh's cup was in my hand, and I took the grapes, squeezed them into Pharaoh's cup and put the cup in Pharaoh's hand." The dream recreates the cupbearer's professional function: take grapes, squeeze into cup, deliver to Pharaoh. The cup (kôs, H3563) is the central object of the cupbearer's ministry — it appears three times in this single verse. The restoration-dream has the cupbearer doing exactly what he is supposed to do. Joseph's interpretation (v13) will confirm that doing one's proper function in Pharaoh's presence = restoration to honor.
12: wayyōʾmer lô yôsēp zeh pitrōnô šĕlōšet haśśārîgîm šĕlōšet yāmîm hēm — "Joseph said, 'This is its interpretation: the three branches are three days.'" Zeh pitrōnô (this is its interpretation) — the confident, direct formula for dream interpretation; no hedging, no conditional language. Šĕlōšet yāmîm hēm (they are three days) — the symbol-to-referent identification: branches = days. The number three recurs in both dreams (three branches, three baskets) and will be fulfilled on the third day (v20). The "third day" as the day of fulfillment is a temporal marker that carries covenantal weight throughout the OT (Gen 22:4; Exod 19:11,16; Hos 6:2).
13: bĕʿôḏ šĕlōšet yāmîm yiśśāʾ parʿōh ʾet-rōʾšĕḵā ûhăšîḇĕḵā ʿal-kannāteḵā wĕnātattā kôs-parʿōh bĕyāḏô — "Within three days Pharaoh will lift up your head (yiśśāʾ... ʾet-rōʾšĕḵā) and restore you to your position." Yiśśāʾ ʾet-rōʾšĕḵā (he will lift your head) — a Hebrew idiom for restoration to honor and position. The same idiom will be used in v19 for the baker's execution — "he will lift off your head" — but with a different verb and referent. The wordplay is dark: both men receive "head-lifting" from Pharaoh, but the type differs entirely. Joseph's positive interpretation comes first, establishing the idiom before inverting it.
14: kî ʾim-zĕḵartanî ʾittĕḵā kaʾăšer yîṭaḇ lāḵ wĕʿāśîtā-nāʾ ʿimmî ḥeseḏ wĕhizkarttanî ʾel-parʿōh wĕhôṣēʾtanî min-habbayit hazzeh — "But remember me when it goes well with you; show me ḥeseḏ and mention me to Pharaoh, and get me out of this house." Joseph's request uses the covenant-vocabulary: zakar (H2142, to remember — the covenant-memory verb; God "remembered" Noah, Rachel, Abraham for Lot) and ḥeseḏ (H2617, covenant-loyalty/lovingkindness). He asks the cupbearer to show the same covenantal faithfulness that YHWH shows to Joseph. Hazzeh (this house) — Joseph refers to the prison as a "house" (bêt hassohar, house of the prison, 39:20) — the same vocabulary as Potiphar's house where YHWH blessed everything. The irony: both houses have become sites of YHWH's presence with Joseph.
15: kî-gunnōḇ gunnabbatî mēʾereṣ hāʿiḇrîm wĕgam-pōh lōʾ-ʿāśîtî mĕʾûmāh kî-śāmû ʾōtî babbôr — "I was indeed stolen (gunnōḇ gunnabbatî) from the land of the Hebrews, and even here I have done nothing to deserve being put in a pit (babbôr)." Gunnōḇ gunnabbatî — the Qal infinitive absolute + perfect (emphatic form): "I was truly stolen/kidnapped." Gānab (H1589, to steal, kidnap) — the Eighth Commandment verb (Exod 20:15). Joseph's self-description as a stolen person positions his slavery as a crime against himself, not a legitimate status. ʾEreṣ hāʿiḇrîm (land of the Hebrews) — the first use of this phrase, identifying Canaan from the perspective of the Egyptian diaspora. Babbôr (the pit/cistern/dungeon, H953) — the same word used for the pit Joseph's brothers threw him into in 37:24 (habôr rēq, the empty pit); the Egyptian prison is linguistically identified with the original abandonment. Joseph's narrative of descent is a single story: pit → Potiphar's prison → pit again.
16: wayyarʾ śar-hāʾōpîm kî ṭôḇ pātar wayyōʾmer ʾel-yôsēp ʾap-ʾănî baḥălōmî wĕhinnēh šĕlōšāh sallê ḥōrî ʿal-rōʾšî — "When the chief baker saw that Joseph had given a favorable interpretation (pātar), he said: 'I also had a dream.'" The baker's disclosure is motivated by the cupbearer's positive outcome — he wants the same. Pātar (H6622, to interpret dreams) — the baker sees that Joseph's interpretation was "good" (ṭôḇ) and decides to share his own dream. The narrative irony is that the baker's eagerness leads him to hear a death sentence. The basket imagery — three baskets on his head — contrasts with the cupbearer's active hand-delivering image; the baker is passive, and the birds eat the food before it reaches Pharaoh.
17: ûḇassal hāʿelyôn mikkōl maʾăḵal parʿōh maʿăśēh ʾōpeh wĕhāʿôp ʾōḵēl ʾōtām min-hassal mēʿal rōʾšî — "In the top basket were all kinds of baked goods for Pharaoh, but the birds were eating them from the basket on my head." The birds eating the food before it reaches Pharaoh is the key detail: in the cupbearer's dream the cup reaches Pharaoh's hand; in the baker's dream the food never reaches Pharaoh at all — intercepted by birds. The baker cannot fulfill his professional function (providing food to Pharaoh) even in his own dream — the dream itself signals a disruption of service that the waking interpretation confirms.
18: wayyaʿan yôsēp wayyōʾmer zeh pitrōnô šĕlōšet hasisallîm šĕlōšet yāmîm hēm — "Joseph answered, 'This is its interpretation: the three baskets are three days.'" The identical interpretation formula — zeh pitrōnô... šĕlōšet yāmîm hēm — as in v12 signals that the pattern is the same. Both dreams involve three items = three days. The parallelism confirms the method of Joseph's interpretation and sets up the contrast: same formula, opposite outcome.
19: bĕʿôḏ šĕlōšet yāmîm yiśśāʾ parʿōh ʾet-rōʾšĕḵā mēʿāleḵā wĕtālāh ʾôtĕḵā ʿal-ʿēṣ wĕʾāḵal hāʿôp ʾet-bĕśārĕḵā mēʿāleḵā — "Within three days Pharaoh will lift off your head (yiśśāʾ... mēʿāleḵā) and impale you on a pole, and the birds will eat your flesh." The same idiom — yiśśāʾ ʾet-rōʾšĕḵā (lift your head) — is used for both interpretation (v13), but here with the preposition mēʿāleḵā (from off you/away from you) — lifting the head away from the body, i.e., decapitation. The birds that ate the food in the baker's dream (v17) will eat the baker's flesh. The wordplay between the two interpretations using identical vocabulary with opposite valence is the narrator's demonstration of Joseph's interpretive precision — the same words apply to both, but the prepositions and referents differ fatally.
20: wayhî bayyôm haššĕlîšî yôm hullelet ʾet-parʿōh wayyaʿaś mišṭeh lĕḵōl-ʿăḇāḏāyw wayyiśśāʾ ʾet-rōʾš śar hammašqîm wĕʾet rōʾš śar hāʾōpîm bĕtôḵ ʿăḇāḏāyw — "On the third day — Pharaoh's birthday (yôm hullelet) — he gave a feast for all his officials and lifted the heads of the chief cupbearer and the chief baker in the presence of his officials." Yôm hullelet ʾet-parʿōh (the day of Pharaoh's birth) — the birthday feast is historically attested for Egyptian practice; the LXX and Josephus confirm this reading. The third day is both the dream-fulfillment day (as Joseph predicted) and the king's birthday — a day of royal clemency and judgment. Both men's "heads are lifted" simultaneously — the idiom fulfilled in its double meaning within a single verse.
21: wayyāšeb ʾet-śar hammašqîm ʿal-mašqēhû wayyittēn hakōs ʿal-kap parʿōh — "He restored the chief cupbearer to his position, so that he put the cup in Pharaoh's hand." The restoration is described in the exact vocabulary of the cupbearer's own dream (v11: "I put the cup in Pharaoh's hand"). The dream-vision and the fulfillment share the identical final image — cup in Pharaoh's hand. This exact correspondence confirms Joseph's interpretive credibility, which will matter in ch 41 when Pharaoh dreams.
22: wĕʾēt śar hāʾōpîm tālāh kaʾăšer pātar lāhem yôsēp — "But he hanged the chief baker, just as Joseph had said." Tālāh (H8518, to hang/impale) — the execution verb. The closing confirmation — kaʾăšer pātar lāhem yôsēp (just as Joseph had interpreted for them) — is the narrator's validation of Joseph's divine-interpretive authority. The fulfillment of both interpretations, one positive and one fatal, proves that Joseph's claims to divine interpretation were true. This double-fulfillment verification sets up the credibility transfer to Pharaoh's own dreams in ch 41.
23: wĕlōʾ-zāḵar śar-hammašqîm ʾet-yôsēp wayyiškāḥēhû — "Yet the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph; he forgot him." The closing double-verb of forgetting — lōʾ-zāḵar (did not remember) + wayyiškāḥēhû (he forgot him) — is the narrative's most poignant moment. Zāḵar (H2142, to remember) is the covenant-memory verb: God "remembered" Noah (8:1), God "remembered" Rachel (30:22), God "remembered" Abraham when Lot was in danger (19:29); Israel is commanded to "remember" (Deut 5:15; 8:2). Human forgetting and divine remembering are the two poles of the covenant memory tradition. The cupbearer forgets; the reader is left with the knowledge that YHWH will remember (41:1: "after two full years, Pharaoh dreamed"). The chapter ends on human failure; the next chapter will open with divine timing.
